speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome to Git Going with GitHub, episode 52: GitHub Desktop. I am Alex. Today we are going to make GitHub Desktop something you can explain, practice, and recover from when the interface surprises you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I am Jamie. I will be the voice of the learner who is willing to ask, what is this for, where am I, and how do I know I did it right?

speaker: ALEX
text: The big idea today: Using GitHub Desktop as an accessible alternative for cloning, branching, committing, and syncing. We will name the concept, explain why it matters, practice the move, and point out the checks that tell you the work is real.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the episode should work even if someone has not read the chapter yet.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The transcript has to stand on its own. It can point toward practice, but it should teach the concept right here in the conversation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, set the room for us. What are we walking into?

speaker: ALEX
text: A Visual Git Client for Every Workflow: Who this is for: You want a dedicated graphical Git application -- something separate from your code editor, with a clear visual interface for cloning, branching, committing, and managing pull requests. The next useful detail is concrete: GitHub Desktop is a great fit if you prefer working with a purpose-built Git GUI, or if VS Code's Source Control panel feels too embedded in the editor for your workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid Git habit is to know which branch you are on, what changed, and what confirmation you expect before you run the next command.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next layer is this. Here is the learner-facing version. GitHub Desktop covers the everyday Git workflow that most contributors use on most days. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the one idea that makes the next few steps less mysterious?

speaker: ALEX
text: What it supports has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: That shows up in the workshop in a few specific ways. Supported: Clone repositories from GitHub. Supported: Create, switch, and delete branches. Supported: Stage files (and individual lines/hunks within files). Supported: Write commit messages and commit. Supported: Push and pull from GitHub. Supported: Open pull requests (launches GitHub.com in your browser).

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels much more doable when you say it as one move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The magic is not speed. The magic is knowing what changed and why it matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now bring the learner back to the room. Anchor this part on What it does not support (use Git CLI or VS Code terminal instead). For these operations, open the repository in your terminal directly from GitHub Desktop -- Repository → Open in Terminal or Repository → Open in Command Prompt puts you in the right directory instantly.

speaker: ALEX
text: For a learner, the useful signals are concrete. Not supported in the GitHub Desktop UI: Interactive rebase (git rebase -i). Not supported in the GitHub Desktop UI: git bisect. Not supported in the GitHub Desktop UI: git clean. Not supported in the GitHub Desktop UI: Creating annotated tags. Not supported in the GitHub Desktop UI: Commit signing (GPG/SSH).

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should someone listen for when a lesson offers more than one tool path?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: What GitHub Desktop Does has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The parts worth keeping in working memory are these. GitHub Desktop handles cloning, branching, staging, committing, and pushing -- all accessible via keyboard and standard form controls. For operations Desktop does not support (rebase, bisect, clean), use Repository then Open in Terminal to land in the right directory instantly. All commit message fields are standard text inputs that screen readers announce as editable text. GitHub Desktop supports system-level zoom and high-contrast OS themes on both Windows and macOS. The diff panel uses green and red backgrounds for additions and deletions -- pair these with font size adjustments in your OS display settings. If the diff colors are hard to distinguish, open the same diff in VS Code via Repository then Open in VS Code for more theme options.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful habit is simple: orient, act, verify, then continue. That pause between action and trust is part of the work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the sequence, because order matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Windows has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, go to desktop.github.com. Then, select "Download for Windows". After that, run the installer -- it installs and launches automatically. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How would you walk the room through that step by step?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, macOS is still useful because linux: GitHub Desktop does not officially support Linux. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Linux users should use VS Code's Source Control panel or the Git CLI.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Go to desktop.github.com. Then: Select "Download for macOS". Next: Open the downloaded.zip, drag GitHub Desktop to your Applications folder. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the talk moves from concept to action. Put 3. Signing In and Authentication into plain language. GitHub Desktop uses browser-based OAuth sign-in -- no tokens or SSH keys needed. The useful version is: It handles authentication for you automatically.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now it sounds like a workflow instead of a wall of instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is where confidence comes from: not from never getting lost, but from knowing how to recover.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Turn that into a path someone can follow.

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. GitHub Desktop stores your credentials securely in the system keychain. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: You won't be asked for a password again.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: Open GitHub Desktop; Select "Sign in to GitHub.com"; Your browser opens to a GitHub authorization page -- sign in with your GitHub account; and Authorize GitHub Desktop when prompted. Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the ordered workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is switch back to GitHub Desktop -- you're signed in. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you keep commands from becoming magic words?

speaker: ALEX
text: This part earns its place because GitHub Desktop has three main areas. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐; │ Toolbar: Current Repository ▾ Current Branch ▾ │; │ Fetch/Push button │; ├─────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┤; │ │ │; │ Left panel: │ Main area: │; │ Changes tab │ File diff. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do we make tool choice feel like access, not pressure?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: The Desktop Interface has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: If someone is taking notes, this is the short list. Use Tab to move between the toolbar, file list, diff pane, and commit area -- each is a distinct focus region. The branch selector is a searchable dropdown: type to filter, Down Arrow to navigate, Enter to select. The commit button is announced as "Commit to [branch name]" -- press Enter to activate. The toolbar at the top stays fixed as you scroll -- repository name, branch, and sync button are always visible. Increase your OS display scaling to enlarge all panels proportionally without breaking the layout. The Changes and History tabs are side-by-side at the top of the left panel -- each tab label is clearly distinct.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the learner do first, second, and then after that?

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the learner-facing version. The easiest way is to go to the repository on GitHub.com and use the Code button.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: On the repository page, activate Code → Open with GitHub Desktop. Then: GitHub Desktop opens with a dialog confirming the URL. Next: Choose where to save the repository on your computer. Last: Select "Clone". If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on From inside GitHub Desktop. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: From inside GitHub Desktop has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Press Ctrl+Shift+O (Windows) or Cmd+Shift+O (macOS) -- "Clone a repository"; Choose the GitHub.com tab to browse your own repositories, or the URL tab to paste any repo URL; Select or type the repository; and Choose a local path. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave From inside GitHub Desktop, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 1 checks: Select "Clone". That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the kind of detail that keeps a screen reader user oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The named thing - the heading, tab, field, branch, or button - is the handhold.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the safe way to learn from that example?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub CLI alternative has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like gh repo clone owner/repo-name; Then open it in GitHub Desktop:; github /path/to/repo. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Create a new branch. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: GitHub Desktop switches to the new branch immediately.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is click Current Branch in the toolbar (or press Ctrl+Shift+N / Cmd+Shift+N). Step two is type a name for your new branch. Step three is select "Create Branch". Step four is choose whether to base it on the current branch or another -- usually keep the default. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Switch to an existing branch. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Switch to an existing branch has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, click Current Branch in the toolbar. Then, type to search or scroll through the branch list. After that, click the branch name -- GitHub Desktop switches and updates your files. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Delete a branch. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Delete a branch is still useful because the branch selector is a dropdown that opens a searchable list. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Screen readers announce the currently selected branch name in the toolbar.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Click Current Branch in the toolbar. Then: Right-click the branch you want to delete. Next: Select "Delete.". Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: ALEX
text: Hold that next to this. Put Reviewing your changes into plain language. When you edit files in your editor and save them, GitHub Desktop automatically detects the changes and shows them in the Changes tab on the left. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here are the anchors worth keeping. The left panel lists every changed file with a checkbox. The right panel shows the diff for the selected file -- red lines removed, green lines added.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learner is not behind if they stop there and check the page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Pausing to verify is not a detour; it is how you keep control of the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid pull request habit is to separate three questions: what changed, why it changed, and what still needs review. That keeps the conversation useful instead of noisy.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I am listening before the workshop starts, what should settle in my mind first?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Every file with a checked checkbox will be included in your next commit.

speaker: ALEX
text: On the ground, that means a few things. Check all -- click the checkbox at the top of the list to stage everything. Uncheck a file to exclude it from the commit (it stays as an uncommitted change).

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Staging individual lines (hunks). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This part earns its place because if a file has multiple changes and you only want to commit some of them.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is select the file in the Changes list. Step two is in the diff view, right-click a line or block. Step three is select "Stage Line" or "Stage Hunk" -- only those lines get included in the commit. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Writing your commit message. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Writing your commit message: GitHub Copilot can help: Not sure what to write? The next useful detail is concrete: Open VS Code alongside GitHub Desktop, open Copilot Chat, and ask: "Write a commit message for these changes: [describe what you changed]." Or use the conventional commit format: feat:, fix:, docs:, refactor.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, in the Summary field at the bottom, type a short commit message (under 72 characters is ideal). Then, optionally add a longer description in the Description field below it. After that, select "Commit to [branch name]" -- your changes are committed locally. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the practical turn. Here is the learner-facing version. After committing, your changes are saved locally but not yet on GitHub. Put another way, the Fetch/Push button in the top-right toolbar handles syncing. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What would you say to someone who is already bracing for this to be too much?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Pushing commits to GitHub: after committing, the button changes to "Push origin" with an upward arrow and the number of commits waiting.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is what that changes in practice. Click "Push origin" -- your commits go to GitHub.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a useful checkpoint before anyone starts pressing keys.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Checkpoints turn uncertainty into information.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the thread going. Anchor this part on Pulling changes from GitHub. When collaborators have pushed new commits, the button shows "Pull origin" with a downward arrow.

speaker: ALEX
text: The room should hear these as checkpoints. Click "Pull origin" -- GitHub Desktop downloads the new commits and updates your local branch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where do you want a learner to place their attention here?

speaker: ALEX
text: Fetching (checking without pulling) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the details that keep the idea from floating away. Click "Fetch origin" -- GitHub Desktop checks for new commits and shows you the count, but doesn't update your files yet. This is safe to do at any time -- it's read-only.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 9. Syncing Your Fork. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Do not treat 9. Syncing Your Fork as decoration. If you forked a repository and the original upstream repo has new commits you want to bring in. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, go to Branch → Merge into current branch. Then, in the branch picker, switch to the "Other branches" tab. After that, select upstream/main (or upstream/master) -- the upstream remote's default branch. Finally, select "Create a merge commit". The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave 9. Syncing Your Fork, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Then push to your fork with "Push origin". Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Setting up the upstream remote (if not already there). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Setting up the upstream remote (if not already there) is still useful because GitHub Desktop adds the upstream remote automatically when you clone a fork from GitHub.com.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Open the repository in terminal: Repository → Open in Terminal. Then: Switch back to GitHub Desktop -- it will now see the upstream branches. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git remote add upstream https://github.com/ORIGINAL-OWNER/ORIGINAL-REPO.git; git fetch upstream. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the part worth saying out loud. Put 10. Resolving Merge Conflicts into plain language. When you pull from GitHub or merge a branch and there are conflicts, GitHub Desktop shows a dialog listing the conflicting files.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that because it gives people permission to slow down.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is the goal. We want the page to feel explorable, not fragile.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Step by step. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Step by step has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the pieces that turn the idea into a usable move. "Open in [your editor]" -- opens the file with conflict markers for manual editing. "Use mine" / "Use theirs" -- accept one entire side of the conflict without editing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: GitHub Desktop shows a "Resolve conflicts before merging" screen; Each conflicting file has two options next to it; For simple conflicts (one side is clearly right), use "Use mine" or "Use theirs"; and For conflicts where you need to keep parts of both, select "Open in [editor]" and resolve manually. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on After resolving in your editor. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This part earns its place because GitHub Copilot can help: Open the conflicting file in VS Code alongside GitHub Desktop. That gives the learner a foothold: place your cursor inside a conflict region and open Copilot Chat (Ctrl+Alt+I, or Chat: Open Chat from the Command Palette if your keymap differs): "Resolve this merge conflict - keep meaningful changes from both sides." Copilot suggests a resolution; you. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is save the file. Step two is switch back to GitHub Desktop -- the file moves from "Conflicting" to "Resolved". Step three is once all conflicts are resolved, select "Continue merge". That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the version that sounds like an instructor, not a manual.

speaker: ALEX
text: 11. Viewing History: The History tab (next to the Changes tab in the left panel) shows every commit on the current branch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Navigating history. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Navigating history has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Click the History tab. Then: Each commit appears as a row: author avatar, commit message, author name, and timestamp. Next: Click any commit to see the full diff on the right -- what changed in each file. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Filtering commits. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Filtering commits: type in the search field above the commit list to filter by commit message, author, or SHA.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the part I would want someone to say out loud while they work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A learner should always know what they are trying to prove before they take the next action.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Viewing a specific file's history. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Viewing a specific file's history has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 2 checks: Right-click any file in your project (via the Files panel in the diff area); and Select "Show in History" -- the commit list filters to only commits that touched that file. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Comparing branches. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Comparing branches has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is click History. Step two is in the branch comparison bar above the commit list, switch to another branch. Step three is GitHub Desktop shows the commits that are different between your current branch and the selected branch. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: ALEX
text: That connects to another useful point. Do not treat 12. Cherry-Pick in GitHub Desktop as decoration. Cherry-pick lets you take a commit from another branch and apply it to your current branch -- without merging the whole branch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How to cherry-pick. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: How to cherry-pick has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Switch to the branch you want to copy the commit FROM (the source branch). Then: Open the History tab. Next: Find the commit you want to copy. Last: Right-click the commit. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave How to cherry-pick, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Select "Cherry-pick commit."; and GitHub Desktop applies the commit and switches to the destination branch. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: ALEX
text: That matters because of the next idea. Put If there's a conflict into plain language. GitHub Desktop shows the conflict resolution screen (same as merging). The useful version is: Resolve the conflicts in your editor, then switch back to GitHub Desktop to continue. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is less about memorizing and more about noticing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Once the learner can name the move, the interface becomes much less intimidating.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 13. Stashing Changes. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Stash saves your uncommitted changes temporarily so you can switch branches or pull without committing half-finished work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Stash your changes. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Stash your changes has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is go to Branch → Stash All Changes (or press Ctrl+Shift+H / Cmd+Shift+H). Step two is your working directory reverts to the last commit. Step three is you can now switch branches safely. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Restore your stash. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Restore your stash: GitHub Desktop supports one stash at a time per branch. The next useful detail is concrete: For multiple stashes, use the Git CLI: git stash list, git stash apply stash@{1}.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, switch back to the branch where you stashed. Then, go to Branch → Pop Stash. After that, your changes come back. Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: ALEX
text: Another way to ground it. Here is the learner-facing version. Go to Edit → Undo Last Commit (or press Ctrl+Z / Cmd+Z right after committing). Put another way, this is equivalent to git reset --soft HEAD 1 -- your changes come back as staged files. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Discard changes to a file. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Discard changes to a file: warning: Discarding changes cannot be undone. That matters in practice: The changes are permanently deleted, not moved to trash.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Right-click a file; Select "Discard Changes."; and Confirm the dialog -- the file reverts to its last committed state. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Discard all changes. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Discard all changes has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 3 checks: Right-click anywhere in the Changes list; Select "Discard All Changes."; and Confirm -- everything in your working directory reverts to the last commit. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 15. Accessibility and Screen Reader Notes. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: GitHub Desktop has partial screen reader support. The listener should be able to check this: Core workflows work well with keyboard navigation; some visual-only elements (like avatar images and some icons) don't have text equivalents.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid navigation habit is to prove where you are before activating controls. Headings, landmarks, and the address bar are not trivia; they are your map.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now slow down for the part people usually miss. Windows -- NVDA / JAWS has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Listen for the small confirmations in this list. Use Tab to move between panels; arrow keys within lists. The commit summary field is reached with Tab after the file list. Conflict dialogs are modal -- screen reader focus moves into the dialog automatically.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on macOS -- VoiceOver. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: macOS -- VoiceOver has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful version is not abstract; it sounds like this. Use Tab and VO+Arrow to navigate panels. The diff view can be explored with VO+Right through the lines. Branch selector opens a searchable popup -- type to filter, Down Arrow to navigate, Return to select.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the moment where the page starts to make sense. Put VS Code as a better accessible alternative into plain language. If screen reader navigation in GitHub Desktop is frustrating for a specific operation, VS Code's Source Control panel is often more accessible. The useful version is: The two tools complement each other -- use whichever works best for the task at hand.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There are a lot of tools in play. How do we keep that from feeling like a contest?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Desktop Accessibility has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: A few details make that real. Commit message fields, branch selector, and conflict resolution buttons are fully keyboard-accessible and announced. The diff panel has partial screen reader support -- for difficult diffs, switch to VS Code's Accessible Diff Viewer (F7 / Alt+F2). Use Shift+F10 on Windows to open context menus via keyboard when right-click actions are needed. File status icons (M, A, D) are small -- increase OS scaling or watch for the filename and status text that accompany them. The conflict resolution dialog uses labeled buttons ("Use mine", "Use theirs") that are easy to identify at any zoom level. If diff colors are indistinguishable at your contrast settings, open the same file in VS Code for richer theme support.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What decision is this helping them make?

speaker: ALEX
text: This part earns its place because GitHub Desktop, VS Code, and the Git CLI all talk to the same Git repository. That gives the learner a foothold: you can switch between them freely -- use GitHub Desktop to stage and commit, then open the terminal for a git rebase -i. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Use the comparison to make a decision, not to recite a table. The main contrasts are: Situation means Best tool. You want a clean visual overview of changes before committing means GitHub Desktop. You want to stage individual lines or hunks means GitHub Desktop or VS Code.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should people carry with them after this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Carry the map. Know what page or tool you are in, know which action you are taking, and know what confirmation should follow. If the confirmation is missing, pause. That pause is not wasted time; it is professional judgment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a better way to say it than just follow the steps.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Steps matter, but understanding wins. That is episode 52. Next in the series is episode 53, where we keep building the same contributor muscles.
